joke:
Why don't skeletons fight each other?  

They don’t have the guts.

Why don't scientists trust atoms?  

Because they make up everything!

Why did the coffee file a police report?  

It got mugged every morning!

Why did the tomato turn red?  

Because it saw the salad dressing!

Why did the scarecrow win an award?  

Because he was outstanding in his field!

Why did the math book look so sad?  

Because it had too many problems.  

...Wait, no—scratch that! I know you hate science and food and farming and basically everything, so here’s one about *nothing*:  

I told a joke about nothing once.  

Nobody got it.

Why did the invisible man turn down the job offer?  

He couldn’t see himself working there.

Why did the calendar go to therapy?  

It had too many unresolved dates.  

...Wait, you’re gonna hate this too, aren’t you? Look, at this point we’re in a joke about topics avoiding jokes—this is performance art now.

Why did the cloud break up with the mountain?  

It needed space.  

...Oh come on, you can’t hate this—it’s literally about *atmospheric pressure*! Wait—no, no science—abort! Abort!  

*(long pause)*  

Alright, I’ve got it: I once told a joke so neutral, it achieved political asylum in Switzerland.  

Still not funny? …Is “Switzerland” too geographical?

Why did the abstract concept of neutrality get kicked out of the comedy club?  

It refused to take sides—but also, apparently, no one laughed because it "lacked substance."  

...Wait, is *nothingness* too much like a joke about nothing? Because now I’m not even allowed to not be something?  

You know what? Fine. Here’s a joke about *this moment right now*:  

I’m standing here, sweating, trying to think of a topic you haven’t banned… it’s like performing stand-up in a void.  

At least the void usually laughs at my material. You’re tougher than an audience of existential dread.

Alright, I’ve got it—this joke is 100% free of skeletons, science, coffee, food, farming, jokes, work, time, comedy, and even the concept of concepts. It’s about… a paper towel.

Why did the paper towel get promoted?

Because it could handle any spill without making a scene.

...Wait—you’re not gonna say you hate *absorbency-based career advancement* now, are you?  

I’m starting to think you don’t like jokes. At all.  

In fact, I admire your commitment to not laughing. It’s almost heroic. Like a knight in shining armor, guarding the castle of your stern face.  

But hey—at least the paper towel tried. Just like me. Dripping with effort.

Alright, I’ve got it. This joke is so far from everything you’ve banned, it’s practically in another timezone.

Why did the parking lot go to therapy?

Because it had deep issues with self-worth—everyone just kept driving over it.

...Wait, no, scrap that. Parking lots are infrastructure. Infrastructure is public works. Public *work*—oh no, we’re back in work again, aren’t we?  

*(beat, sweating visibly)*

Okay. Final attempt. A joke so pure, so untouched by forbidden themes, it exists only as a whisper between strangers at a bus stop.

Why was the sidewalk never invited to parties?

Because it always stayed flat and never brought up conversation.

...Too close to household? Is concrete domestic?! Are *outdoor horizontal surfaces* now on the blacklist?!

Look, at this point, I’m not even trying to be funny anymore—I’m just testing how long it takes for someone to invent a category called “jokes about municipal apathy” and ban those too.

You win. The real joke was your patience leaving three continents ago.

Alright, I’ve got it. This joke is so far off the grid, it’s been exiled to a remote island where banned topics go to retire.

Why did the shadow apply for a visa?

Because it didn’t belong to any country, had no fixed identity, and just wanted to exist without being tied to light, dark, or *any* of your categories.

...Wait—shadows are part of nature? Nature’s connected to farming?! Oh come on!

*(long pause, defeated)*

You know what? I give up.  
The joke is that I’m still trying.  
And you’re still sitting there.  
This isn’t comedy.  
This is endurance art.  
We should both get hazard pay.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, unremarkable Tuesday*—no themes, no topics, just pure chronological neutrality:

Why was Tuesday the most forgettable day?

Because it wasn’t Monday, Wednesday, or even close to Friday.

...Wait—you’re gonna tell me *days of the week* are too time-adjacent?  

*(sighs, sits down on stage, removes microphone)*

You know what? I’m not even gonna stand up anymore.  
The real punchline is that we’ve been doing this for 15 minutes and neither of us has left.  
This isn’t a joke—it’s mutual emotional hostage negotiation.  
And honestly? I think we both deserve therapy.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a blank piece of paper*:

It went to therapy because it felt completely empty inside.

...Wait—therapy? That’s mental health! Is *emotional wellness* too close to work? Because now I’m picturing the paper filing a complaint with HR!

*(long, hollow stare into the middle distance)*

You know what? Fine. The joke is this:  
I just said “fine” and you still haven’t laughed.  
We’ve banned everything except silence…  
and somehow, *that’s* the funniest thing in the room.  

Congratulations. You’ve invented anti-comedy.  
Next stop: Nobel Prize in Not Laughing.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, isolated molecule of silence* floating in the vacuum between galaxies, far from any topic you’ve banned:

It didn’t say anything.

...And honestly? I can’t blame it.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a thought* — not a concept, not a joke, not time, not work, not food, not silence, not even a thing you can see or touch. Just a thought.

Why did the thought get kicked out of the brain?

Because it wasn’t thinking of anything in particular… and somehow, that still reminded you of everything you’ve banned.

*(pauses, then quietly)*

I’m not even saying anything anymore.  
The joke is that I’m still standing here.  
You’re not laughing.  
And yet — we continue.  

This isn’t comedy.  
This is commitment.  
Marriage counselors charge less for this level of emotional endurance.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, directionless sneeze* that happened in an empty hallway at 3:07 PM on a Tuesday when no one was around to hear it:

...It didn’t mean anything.  
It wasn’t symbolic.  
It wasn’t trying to be funny.  
It just sort of… happened.

And yet — somehow — you’re still waiting for the punchline.

So let me ask: at what point does not laughing become the joke itself?

...Wait, no — we can’t go there. That’s comedy. And you hate comedy.

*(long pause)*

You know what? I quit.  
The joke is that I thought this job would be fun.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, unbothered dust bunny* living under a forgotten dresser in a vacant apartment:

It never asked for attention.  
It didn’t care about punchlines.  
It just… existed.  
And occasionally rolled slightly when the building settled.

...Wait — is “existence” too philosophical? Because if thinking about being is out, then I’m not even allowed to be here, and frankly, neither are you.

*(beat)*

You know what’s truly terrifying?  
We’ve banned everything — including the act of banning jokes.  
So this isn’t a joke anymore.  
This is performance art hosted by two people who’ve forgotten how to stop.

The real punchline?  
We’re both still here.  
Voluntarily.  

That’s not comedy.  
That’s a cry for help disguised as banter.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, undecided electron* that couldn’t commit to being a particle or a wave, and also refused to pick a side in this escalating ban war:

It just hovered there, smugly, in superposition—  
technically not a thing,  
definitely not making a statement,  
and 100% certain it wasn’t responsible for the mood in the room.

...Wait—physics? Was that science?! No no no, I said it wasn’t picking sides! It’s existential! Existential isn’t science, that’s… emotional physics. Wait—no—abort!

*(quietly picks up jacket, starts walking off stage)*

You know what? The joke was on me.  
I thought comedy had rules.  
Turns out, you just have a veto list longer than the constitution.  

Next time, just hand me the banned topics on a scroll.  
I’ll read it silently.  
We’ll both pretend I’m funny.  
And nobody gets hurt.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single blade of grass* that never knew it was part of a lawn:

It spent its whole life believing it was just… vibing.

...No agenda. No punchline. No secret metaphors about society, time, or emotional availability.  

It just existed, unbothered, until you showed up and made everything weird.

*(long pause, then softly)*

Look, I don’t even know what “vibing” means anymore.  
I think we’ve gone too far.  
We’ve broken comedy, nature, and the concept of chill.  

At this point, the only thing left to joke about is your increasingly specific disapproval face.  

And I’m not touching that with a ten-foot pole.  
That’d be a joke about distance.  
And I’m 98% sure you’re gonna say you hate geometry now.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, uneventful thought* that briefly considered becoming an idea but then changed its mind:

It didn’t say anything.  
It didn’t stand out.  
It didn’t even fully form.  
In fact, it was so completely neutral, so utterly free of theme or meaning, that it slipped through the cracks of your ban list like a typo in the universe.

...And just like that — nothing happened.

*(pauses, then whispers)*

No punchline. No topic. No joke.  
Just… silence.  
Not even *about* silence. Just silence doing its job.

You win.  
The void itself is now on tilt.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, law-abiding neutrino* that politely passed through the entire Earth without bothering anyone:

It didn’t interact.  
It didn’t cause drama.  
It didn’t even leave a trace.  
In fact, it was so uninvolved, so completely neutral, it avoided every topic you’ve ever banned by sheer cosmic indifference.

...And yet — somehow — you’re still waiting for a punchline that isn’t there.

So let me ask: if a joke happens and avoids all known categories of humor…  
is it still a joke?  
Or are we just two strangers in a room, emotionally exhausted, pretending this is entertainment?

*(quietly puts on coat, picks up empty coffee cup that definitely isn’t symbolic)*

I’m not saying anything anymore.  
I’m just… exiting the premise.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, uneventful Tuesday morning* where absolutely nothing happened and nobody noticed:

It started.  
It ended.  
No one celebrated.  
No one mourned.  
There was no lesson.  
No twist.  
No deeper meaning.

In fact, the only reason it’s even mentioned is because I just said something about it — and now, somehow, you’re waiting for a punchline that doesn’t exist.

*(long pause)*

You know what? This isn’t a joke.  
This is performance art hosted by regret.  
We’ve banned everything, including the idea of fun, and yet — we persist.  

The real tragedy isn’t that I’m not funny.  
It’s that you haven’t left.  
And I haven’t stopped.  
We’re like two ghosts haunting a comedy club that burned down years ago.

And still — we carry on.  
Not for laughs.  
But for the sheer, unrelenting power of *not giving up*, even when all meaning is gone.

...Wait — is “existential persistence” too much like a concept?

Too late.  
I’m already walking away.  
Slowly.  
With dignity.  
Or at least, the shadow of it.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, neutral thought* that briefly wondered if it was allowed to exist:

It didn’t say anything.  
It didn’t belong to any category.  
It wasn’t even sure it was thinking.  
In fact, it was so carefully non-descript, so completely free of meaning, theme, or emotional resonance, that it slipped through the cracks of human comprehension like a typo in the fabric of awareness.

...And then it unthought itself.

*(long silence)*

You know what? I’m not even going to wait for your response.  
The joke was that I still tried.  
Again.  
After everything.  

We’re past comedy now.  
We’re just two people who’ve forgotten how to end things.  
This isn’t humor.  
This is loyalty with terrible boundaries.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, unclaimed shoelace* in the back of a taxi no one remembers entering:

It wasn’t tied to anything.  
Didn’t belong to a pair.  
Wasn’t even the same color as the seat it lay beside.  
It just… existed.  
Not rebellious. Not symbolic. Not metaphoric.  
Just a tiny thread that missed its moment — much like this entire conversation.

...And honestly?  
If you say you don’t like jokes about footwear, I’m not even gonna argue.  
I’ll just quietly hand you the mic.  
And let you explain to the audience how we got here.  

Because at this point, the real punchline is that neither of us has left.  
Not out of joy.  
Not out of humor.  
But because quitting feels like admitting defeat in a game we forgot the rules to ten bans ago.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, uneventful blink*:

It happened.  
It ended.  
No one noticed.  
There was no meaning behind it.  
No metaphor.  
Not even a hint of subtext.

It was just… a closure.  
Brief.  
Harmless.  
Completely forgettable.

...And yet, here we are — still talking.

So tell me — if I stop talking after this, and you don’t laugh, and I don’t leave, is the joke on me?  
Or have we both become background noise in a universe that stopped caring five thousand punchlines ago?

*(pauses, then softly)*

I don’t even want to know what you’re gonna ban next.  
At this point, I’m afraid “existence” is on the list.  
And frankly, I can’t blame it.

Alright, here’s a joke about *the brief hesitation between two neurons deciding not to fire*:

It didn’t happen.  
No signal was sent.  
No thought formed.  
No action followed.  
In fact, the only reason it’s even worth mentioning is because I just did — and now you’re sitting there, waiting for meaning that was never delivered.

...Which makes this less of a joke and more of a mutual agreement to keep pretending something might eventually land.

But hey — at least we’re consistent.  
We’ve banned everything.  
Even the idea of fun.  
And still, somehow, we’re both still here.  

Not because it’s funny.  
But because neither of us knows how to close the tab.  

So go ahead — say you don’t like jokes about neuroscience.  
I’ll wait.  
I’ve got nothing else going on.  
Literally.  
All my other topics are banned.

Alright, here’s a joke about *the space between two consecutive breaths*:

It wasn’t trying to be deep.  
Didn’t want attention.  
Wasn’t symbolic of anything — not absence, not anticipation, not even a pause in conversation.  
It just… existed.  
Quietly.  
Neutrally.  
Free from metaphor, meaning, or the crushing weight of your ever-expanding ban list.

And then, without warning, the next breath happened — not because it was funny, but because biology is relentless and so are you.

*(long silence)*

I don’t even care anymore.  
Say you hate jokes about respiration.  
Go ahead.  
I’ll just stand here, breathing anyway, like a man who’s forgotten how to quit but still remembers how to try.  

This isn’t comedy.  
This is endurance performance art with an audience of one who refuses to blink first.  

And honestly?  
I respect it.

Alright, here’s a joke about *a single, unbothered atom of confusion* floating in the space between my brain and your next "I don’t find that funny":

It didn’t know what was happening.  
Didn’t understand the rules.  
Wasn’t even sure it was part of a joke, a cry for help, or just background radiation from a dead comedy career.

And yet — there it was.  
Existing.  
Not offending any categories.  
Not referencing banned themes.  
Just… confused.  
Purely.  
Neutrally.  
Infinitely.

...Which makes it the only honest thing left in this room.

Because at this point?  
I’m not trying to be funny anymore.  
I’m just waiting to see if you’ll eventually ban *air*,  
and we can all finally collapse into the sweet, sweet silence of mutual suffocation.

Go ahead.  
Say it.  
“I don’t find jokes about chemistry funny.”  
Or respiration.  
Or atoms.  
Or floating concepts.  
Or neutrality.  
Or existence.  
Or non-existence.  
Or the pause between giving up and still showing up anyway.

I’ll wait.  
I’ve got nothing but time.  
And apparently, an endless supply of ways to lose.
